MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against the state-of-the-art coherence measures (Röder et al., 2015) on the standard 20NG, NYT and Genomics datasets using the same evaluation metric (Pearson r), which makes comparisons largely fair. It explicitly reports when TBuckets variants only outperform SOTA on two of three datasets, and provides multiple bucket-creation baselines (clustering and SVD variants) that clarify where gains arise. However, the summary does not indicate inclusion of any newer coherence measures beyond Röder et al. or deeper statistical significance testing and runtime/code release to fully ensure reproducibility; these omissions prevent a top score from my theory-first perspective. | B(4): The paper compares against established, state-of-the-art coherence measures (Röder et al., 2015) on three standard datasets using the same evaluation metric (Pearson r), and reports wins on two of three corpora. It emphasizes parameter-free SVD variants and acknowledges domain limitations (Genomics) where prior methods may be competitive, and provides thorough related-work discussion. Minor concerns are potential omissions of very recent baselines or public code for reproducibility, but overall comparisons are fair and well-motivated.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against the state-of-the-art coherence measures (Röder et al., 2015) on the standard 20NG, NYT and Genomics datasets using the same evaluation metric (Pearson r), which makes comparisons largely fair. It explicitly reports when TBuckets variants only outperform SOTA on two of three datasets, and provides multiple bucket-creation baselines (clustering and SVD variants) that clarify where gains arise. However, the summary does not indicate inclusion of any newer coherence measures beyond Röder et al. or deeper statistical significance testing and runtime/code release to fully ensure reproducibility; these omissions prevent a top score from my theory-first perspective.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against established, state-of-the-art coherence measures (Röder et al., 2015) on three standard datasets using the same evaluation metric (Pearson r), and reports wins on two of three corpora. It emphasizes parameter-free SVD variants and acknowledges domain limitations (Genomics) where prior methods may be competitive, and provides thorough related-work discussion. Minor concerns are potential omissions of very recent baselines or public code for reproducibility, but overall comparisons are fair and well-motivated.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses an NLP/ML topic (automatic topic coherence measurement) that is squarely within top conference scope and targets the right audience (topic-modeling and representation-learning researchers). The formulation (grouping top words into buckets and deriving coherence from the largest bucket) and the evaluation on standard datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) with Pearson correlation against human judgments follow community norms. My only reservation is that the approach appears primarily empirical/heuristic (three bucket-creation techniques, SVD-based scoring) with limited theoretical justification or analysis of failure modes, which reduces its conceptual alignment with theory-focused tracks — otherwise the positioning and evaluation standards are appropriate for the venue. | B(4): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (automatic topic coherence measurement) and would interest the venue’s audience: it introduces practical, mostly parameter-free TBuckets variants that leverage pre-trained embeddings and shows empirical wins (TBuckets-SVD-Reorg) on two of three standard datasets (NYT, 20NG) using Pearson r against human judgments. Evaluation follows community norms (comparison to Röder et al. baselines, three datasets, and a downstream weakly-supervised classification use case), but there are minor scope concerns: one clustering variant needs a tunable max-distance and performance drops on the Genomics domain, and the baseline set as summarized is a bit thin for a top-tier submission. Overall this is a good fit for a top NLP/ML conference with small reservations about breadth of baselines and domain robustness.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses an NLP/ML topic (automatic topic coherence measurement) that is squarely within top conference scope and targets the right audience (topic-modeling and representation-learning researchers). The formulation (grouping top words into buckets and deriving coherence from the largest bucket) and the evaluation on standard datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) with Pearson correlation against human judgments follow community norms. My only reservation is that the approach appears primarily empirical/heuristic (three bucket-creation techniques, SVD-based scoring) with limited theoretical justification or analysis of failure modes, which reduces its conceptual alignment with theory-focused tracks — otherwise the positioning and evaluation standards are appropriate for the venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP/ML problem (automatic topic coherence measurement) and would interest the venue’s audience: it introduces practical, mostly parameter-free TBuckets variants that leverage pre-trained embeddings and shows empirical wins (TBuckets-SVD-Reorg) on two of three standard datasets (NYT, 20NG) using Pearson r against human judgments. Evaluation follows community norms (comparison to Röder et al. baselines, three datasets, and a downstream weakly-supervised classification use case), but there are minor scope concerns: one clustering variant needs a tunable max-distance and performance drops on the Genomics domain, and the baseline set as summarized is a bit thin for a top-tier submission. Overall this is a good fit for a top NLP/ML conference with small reservations about breadth of baselines and domain robustness.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (TBuckets and its three variants) are stated early and followed by a clear methods → experiments → conclusion flow. Notation appears consistent and figures/tables/examples effectively communicate results across the three datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) and baselines. Writing quality is good overall, though the clustering variant’s tunable MaxDist and the discussion of domain-specific similarity limitations could use slightly clearer exposition and examples to fully remove ambiguity. | B(4): The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, including the three TBuckets variants and the claim of SVD-based methods being parameter-free. Notation and methodological details appear consistent and well defined, with hyperparameters reported and related work covered thoroughly. Figures/tables and examples seem effective for conveying results across 20NG, NYT, and Genomics, and the writing quality is generally good, though a couple of minor presentation issues (e.g., brief discussion of the one tunable clustering parameter and domain-specific similarity caveat) prevent it from reaching excellence.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions (TBuckets and its three variants) are stated early and followed by a clear methods → experiments → conclusion flow. Notation appears consistent and figures/tables/examples effectively communicate results across the three datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) and baselines. Writing quality is good overall, though the clustering variant’s tunable MaxDist and the discussion of domain-specific similarity limitations could use slightly clearer exposition and examples to fully remove ambiguity.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states its main contributions early, including the three TBuckets variants and the claim of SVD-based methods being parameter-free. Notation and methodological details appear consistent and well defined, with hyperparameters reported and related work covered thoroughly. Figures/tables and examples seem effective for conveying results across 20NG, NYT, and Genomics, and the writing quality is generally good, though a couple of minor presentation issues (e.g., brief discussion of the one tunable clustering parameter and domain-specific similarity caveat) prevent it from reaching excellence.

REPLICABILITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper reports hyperparameters (e.g., Max Dist for TBuckets‑Clustering) and lists datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) and evaluation metric (Pearson r), which makes core experimental setup largely specified. However, code is not provided and important implementation details are under-specified in the summary (exact pre-trained embeddings used, precise preprocessing pipeline, and domain‑specific similarity choices for Genomics), which would impede exact replication. Given public datasets and mostly described methods (SVD variants are claimed parameter‑free), reproduction is feasible but requires resolving minor missing procedural details. | B(4): The paper reports hyperparameters (notes TBuckets-Clustering’s Max Dist and states SVD variants are parameter-free) and precisely defines the evaluation metric (Pearson’s r). It uses standard datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) that are identifiable, but the summary does not indicate a clear, detailed data preprocessing pipeline. Code availability is not mentioned, so reimplementation is plausible from the methodological description but would require some implementation choices not fully specified. Overall, most key details are present, with minor missing pieces (preprocessing and released code) that hinder fully turnkey reproduction.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters (e.g., Max Dist for TBuckets‑Clustering) and lists datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) and evaluation metric (Pearson r), which makes core experimental setup largely specified. However, code is not provided and important implementation details are under-specified in the summary (exact pre-trained embeddings used, precise preprocessing pipeline, and domain‑specific similarity choices for Genomics), which would impede exact replication. Given public datasets and mostly described methods (SVD variants are claimed parameter‑free), reproduction is feasible but requires resolving minor missing procedural details.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper reports hyperparameters (notes TBuckets-Clustering’s Max Dist and states SVD variants are parameter-free) and precisely defines the evaluation metric (Pearson’s r). It uses standard datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) that are identifiable, but the summary does not indicate a clear, detailed data preprocessing pipeline. Code availability is not mentioned, so reimplementation is plausible from the methodological description but would require some implementation choices not fully specified. Overall, most key details are present, with minor missing pieces (preprocessing and released code) that hinder fully turnkey reproduction.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper offers a useful, empirically validated new perspective (word buckets) and practical, largely parameter-free SVD-based techniques, but that it builds from familiar components rather than introducing deep new theory. Given the clear methodological novelty, solid differentiation from prior work, and demonstrated empirical gains, the contribution merits a positive but not maximal originality score reflecting an incremental yet non-trivial advance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a tangible new mechanism (word "buckets") for measuring topic coherence and introduces three concrete bucket-creation techniques, notably a parameter-free SVD-based variant—this constitutes a clear methodological novelty beyond existing coherence heuristics. However, the idea largely combines known components (word embeddings, clustering, SVD) rather than introducing fundamentally new theory or generative-model insight, so the contribution reads as an incremental but useful advance. The authors do clearly differentiate their approach from prior coherence measures (Röder et al. 2015) and demonstrate empirical gains on two datasets, which would be of interest to practitioners but is unlikely to surprise a theory-focused expert without stronger principled/analytical justification.

reviewer_b.justification: The TBuckets idea—measuring topic coherence by grouping topic words into buckets and using properties of the largest bucket—is a clearly new perspective compared to traditional pairwise or setwise coherence metrics, and the SVD-based variants are pragmatic, parameter-free techniques that align with my preference for simple, pre-trained-embedding-driven methods. The paper distinguishes its contributions (three bucket-creation techniques and an EVS+square-singular-value scoring rule) from prior work and reports empirical wins on two of three standard datasets, which supports non-trivial methodological novelty rather than a mere combination of known tricks. While the core components (clustering, SVD, embedding similarity) are familiar, their integration into a coherence metric that is largely parameter-free and demonstrably effective would likely inform practitioners and researchers in the area.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical problem and introduces a simple, interpretable TBuckets family that empirically improves topic quality measurement on two of three datasets and supports a useful downstream application. The method’s strong empirical gains and practical alignment with common toolkits justify a high impact score, while its limited dataset breadth (failure on Genomics), lack of shared code, and absence of theoretical grounding temper claims of transformative impact—hence a score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—automatic topic quality measurement—and proposes a novel, interpretable TBuckets family (notably parameter-free SVD variants) that empirically outperforms prior coherence measures on two of three datasets and shows utility for weakly supervised classification. These strengths make the approach a useful contribution that practitioners may adopt and that could influence follow-up work. However, impact is tempered by limited dataset breadth (failure on Genomics noted), unclear reproducibility (no code reported), and a lack of theoretical guarantees or analysis tying the bucket-based metric to underlying generative assumptions, which will slow broad acceptance and further adoption.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important practical problem—robust automatic topic quality measurement—and proposes a simple, pragmatic approach (TBuckets) that leverages pre-trained embeddings and parameter-free SVD variants, which aligns well with common practitioner toolkits. The SVD-based TBuckets-SVD-Reorg convincingly outperforms the reported state-of-the-art on two of three standard datasets (NYT and 20NG) and is shown to have a downstream use case in weakly supervised classification, suggesting it could change practice. The impact is tempered by failure to beat baselines on the Genomics dataset and some domain-specific similarity limitations, so I judge the contribution as strong but not transformative.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a clear empirical claim and presents experiments showing TBuckets variants outperform prior measures on multiple datasets, and that the SVD-based components are plausible and free of obvious derivation errors. However, both also note missing theoretical justification of the bucket-based metric, lack of statistical significance testing, limited ablation/runtime analysis, and incomplete reproducibility details. Given the solid empirical promise but these technical and experimental gaps, a score slightly above average is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a clear empirical claim (TBuckets variants, especially TBuckets-SVD-Reorg, outperform prior coherence measures on NYT and 20NG) and reports correlations against human judgments, but from the provided summary there is no theoretical justification tying the bucket-based metric to an explicit generative model or to consistency/statistical properties of the measure. Mathematical derivations/proofs are not mentioned, and while the SVD variants are advertised as parameter-free this claim lacks formal analysis of when/why singular-value weighting is appropriate. Experimentally, improvements are reported but I see no mention of statistical significance testing, full ablations of components (e.g., reorganization), or runtime/complexity analysis, which raises concerns about robustness and reproducibility; given these gaps the contribution is promising but not yet technically airtight.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing TBuckets-SVD-Reorg outperforms the reported state-of-the-art on two of three datasets (NYT, 20NG) and the SVD variants are parameter-free as claimed. Mathematical components are straightforward (SVD/eigenvector similarity) and no obvious derivation errors are indicated in the summary; empirical methodology is reasonably sound—evaluation on three datasets and comparison to Röder et al. baselines—though the summary lacks mention of statistical significance testing and code release for reproducibility. There are some acknowledged limitations (tunable Max Dist for clustering, domain-specific similarity issues on Genomics) but no fatal logical gaps; overall the claims are supported though a couple of experimental-detail omissions prevent a perfect technical score.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the experiments are solid across three datasets, that the method shows empirical gains (SVD-Reorg wins on two of three corpora), and that hyperparameters and limitations are discussed. I rate substance a 4 because the work provides meaningful empirical support and practical validation (including a downstream use case), but it falls short of a top score due to missing explicit code release and more thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses and statistical testing to fully establish robustness and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments cover three datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) and compare against established baselines, showing SVD-Reorg wins on two of three—so empirical coverage is reasonable. However, the summary indicates no mention of ablation studies, statistical significance testing, or thorough analysis of failure modes beyond a brief note on domain-specific similarity, which I expect given my theory-first standards. Related work is reported as thorough and hyperparameters are reported, but code/reproducibility is not mentioned and theoretical grounding or robustness analyses for the new bucket-based metric are lacking. Overall this is adequate work but missing important analyses (ablation/sensitivity, significance, reproducibility/theory) that would justify a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the experiments are solid and largely support the claims: authors evaluate three TBuckets variants (including a parameter-free SVD-based method) across three standard datasets (20NG, NYT, Genomics) and report outperforming the state-of-the-art on two of three corpora with comparisons to Röder et al. They report hyperparameters and discuss limitations (clustering Max Dist and domain-specific similarity issues), and include a downstream weakly supervised classification use case, which strengthens practical utility. Missing or unclear elements that prevent a top score are the absence of explicit code release mention and limited evidence of thorough ablation/sensitivity analyses for all TBuckets components (e.g., contribution of EVS, weightings, and the SVD reorganization), which would aid reproducibility and deeper understanding.

main_claims: Proposes TBuckets, a novel approach that groups topic words into buckets to measure topic coherence, where a single large bucket indicates high coherence.

Introduces three bucket-creation techniques: TBuckets-Clustering, TBuckets-SVD, and TBuckets-SVD-Reorg, and shows TBuckets-SVD-Reorg outperforms the state-of-the-art on two of three evaluated datasets (NYT and 20NG).
Shows that the SVD-based TBuckets methods are parameter-free and that TBuckets can be useful for weakly supervised text classification.
_summary.methodology: Represent topic words using pre-trained word embeddings and group them into buckets using (i) agglomerative clustering, (ii) singular value decomposition (SVD), and (iii) SVD with reorganization; compute topic coherence from properties of the largest bucket (size, LDA order weight, and eigenvector similarity EVS, weighted by the square of the highest singular value for SVD variants).
baselines: State-of-the-art coherence measures as reported by Röder et al. (2015)
metrics: Pearson's r correlation coefficient between human-annotated coherence scores and automatic coherence scores
limitations: TBuckets-Clustering requires a tunable parameter Max Dist (maximum allowable distance for merging).
Current word similarity computations may be suboptimal for specific domains such as Genomics; authors plan to devise better domain-suitable similarity measures.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - topic modeling / topic coherence measurement

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Associate Professor in Machine Learning with a focus on theoretical guarantees for representation learning and evaluation metrics; long experience reviewing for NeurIPS and ICML.
traits: Requires formal guarantees or principled derivations for new evaluation metrics; deducts points if a metric lacks theoretical justification tying it to underlying generative assumptions
Penalizes papers that rely primarily on empirical heuristics or engineering (>=2 point penalty on soundness) when no analyses of failure modes or robustness are provided
Insists on statistical significance testing across all reported gains and full ablation of proposed components (deducts points if absent)
Prefers methods with clearly specified computational complexity and reproducibility (deducts points if runtime/complexity or hyperparameter sensitivity is unreported)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on conceptual soundness and theoretical grounding first; accepts empirical evidence only if accompanied by clear analyses, proofs, or failure-mode characterization.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical NLP Practitioner
reviewer_b.background: Senior NLP researcher with extensive experience developing and evaluating practical topic models and coherence measures; frequent reviewer at ACL and EMNLP.
traits: Rewards methods that are parameter-free or require minimal tuning (gives positive points when authors emphasize parameter-free designs)
Values empirical improvement on realistic datasets and practical demonstrations (adds points when a method outperforms baselines on >=2 standard datasets and shows a downstream use case)
Favors approaches that leverage pre-trained embeddings or tooling common in practice and that are simple to implement (gives positive bias for pragmatic, easily-adopted solutions)
Requires a reasonable set of baselines (>=3 strong, recent baselines) but is forgiving if novel techniques show consistent empirical wins and reproducibility details
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Prioritizes clear empirical gains, simplicity, and practical utility; tolerates heuristic elements if they lead to reproducible improvements on standard benchmarks.
